required_changes: Assign an explicit top-level CIDR block for the AWS VPC: GCP networks have no VPC-level CIDR, so one must be chosen wide enough to contain every subnet being migrated
Recreate GCP's implicit global routing scope as a single-region AWS VPC; multi-region GCP networks require one AWS VPC per target region plus explicit peering
rules.rationale: A GCP VPC network and an AWS VPC serve the same isolation-boundary role, but GCP's network is global while AWS VPCs are regional. The global-to-regional collapse is the only structural gap, and it is fully resolved by the CIDR-block and per-region decisions above.

required_changes: GCP subnets are regional; AWS subnets are zonal — each GCP subnet must be split into one AWS subnet per availability zone it needs to reach
private_ip_google_access has no AWS analog; equivalent private connectivity to AWS-managed services is provided by VPC endpoints, which must be provisioned separately
rules.rationale: Direct conceptual equivalent, but the regional (GCP) vs zonal (AWS) scope inverts the forward direction's simplification: migrating to AWS typically INCREASES subnet count, since each region-wide GCP subnet becomes multiple zone-scoped AWS subnets.

required_changes: GCP firewall rules are VPC-level and target instances via network tags or service accounts; AWS security groups are instance-attached, so each tag-targeted rule becomes a security group applied to every matching instance
GCP's default-deny-ingress / allow-all-egress posture must be made explicit: AWS security groups require every allowed egress path to be listed, not just ingress
manual_actions: Review the generated security-group boundaries: GCP tag-to-tag rules (source/target tags) have no direct AWS equivalent and must be re-expressed as security-group-to-security-group references
rules.rationale: Both enforce L3/L4 network policy, but the attachment model differs fundamentally (network-level tagged rules vs per-instance stateful groups). Automatable for simple CIDR-based rules; tag-to-tag reference graphs need human review, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: GCP images cannot be booted directly on EC2: rebuild the OS image via an AMI bakery pipeline or import it with AWS Application Migration Service
Map GCP machine types (e.g. e2-medium) to EC2 instance types (e.g. t3.medium) by vCPU/memory profile
GCP startup-script metadata becomes EC2 user_data
An attached GCP service account becomes an EC2 instance profile (IAM role)
rules.rationale: VM-to-VM is the most mechanically translatable workload class. Image conversion is solved tooling, and machine-type mapping is a lookup table — differences are numerous but each is individually automatable.

required_changes: Bucket names are globally unique in both clouds but the namespaces are separate: verify the target S3 name is available or apply a rename convention
Cloud Storage IAM bindings and uniform bucket-level access must be re-expressed as S3 bucket policies and ACLs
GCS storage classes map to S3 classes (STANDARD/NEARLINE->STANDARD_IA/COLDLINE->GLACIER/ARCHIVE->DEEP_ARCHIVE)
rules.rationale: Object storage semantics are near-identical between GCS and S3; AWS DataSync or S3 batch operations handle data movement natively. Access-policy translation is rule-driven (IAM bindings -> S3 policy JSON).

required_changes: Verify engine/version parity: Cloud SQL engine versions do not always have a matching RDS version
Cloud SQL's Private Service Access requirement disappears: RDS private connectivity uses a DB subnet group inside the existing VPC instead — this is a simplification, not an added dependency
Cloud SQL database flags become RDS parameter groups (not all parameters have equivalents)
manual_actions: Plan the data migration itself (AWS Database Migration Service or dump/restore) including a cutover window — infrastructure translation does not move data
rules.rationale: Managed-SQL to managed-SQL is conceptually clean but operationally the highest-risk resource class: it carries state and needs a data-migration plan outside Terraform's scope. PARTIAL, not SUPPORTED, precisely because the infrastructure translation alone is insufficient.

required_changes: A GCP service account plus its project/resource-level IAM bindings decomposes into an AWS IAM role with a trust policy and one or more attached permission policies
GCP predefined/custom roles have no 1:1 AWS managed-policy equivalents; each role's effective permissions must be mapped to the nearest managed policy or a customer-managed policy
manual_actions: Security review of every translated role: permission models differ enough (IAM conditions, resource hierarchies, service-specific permissions) that automated mapping risks silent privilege escalation or breakage
Redesign trust relationships: GCP service-account impersonation and Workload Identity Federation become AWS AssumeRole trust policies (and IAM Roles Anywhere for external federation)
rules.rationale: IAM is where the two clouds' models diverge most. Mechanical translation is possible for trivial roles but dangerous at scale — an over-permissive mapping is a security incident, an under-permissive one is an outage. MANUAL by policy: the platform proposes, a human approves.

required_changes: GCP's five-resource LB stack (forwarding rule, target proxy, URL map, backend service, health check) collapses into three AWS resources (load balancer, listener, target group) — this is an N:1 translation
GCP's passthrough Network Load Balancer maps to an AWS Network Load Balancer instead of the ALB stack; the load_balancer_type native attribute determines which target type applies
Google-managed or self-managed SSL certificates become ACM certificates or imported certificates attached to the listener
manual_actions: Validate URL-map path/host rules against the generated ALB listener rules: complex routing (header/query conditions) may need restructuring to fit ALB's rule-condition model
rules.rationale: Functionally equivalent capability, structurally very different composition. The N:1 collapse is fully rule-expressible, but routing-rule fidelity for complex URL maps warrants human validation, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: Cloud NAT is router-based (one config per Cloud Router, covering a whole region); AWS requires one NAT gateway per availability zone for equivalent redundancy
Cloud NAT's auto-allocated or reserved IPs become AWS Elastic IPs, which must be allocated explicitly and attached to each NAT gateway
rules.rationale: Both provide managed NAT for private instances. AWS's per-AZ NAT gateway model is more granular than GCP's regional Cloud-Router attachment, which typically increases the resource count on this side of the migration.

required_changes: Tunnel configuration parameters (IKE version, shared secret, BGP config) must be re-established on the AWS side
GCP HA VPN's 2-tunnels-per-gateway-interface model becomes AWS's 2-tunnels-per-connection model — tunnel count is preserved but the grouping differs
manual_actions: Coordinate VPN cutover with the on-premises network team to avoid connectivity gaps
rules.rationale: IPsec VPN is standardized across clouds. Configuration differs but the protocol is the same, making this a well-understood migration path.

required_changes: GCP peering is bi-directional by a single configuration; AWS requires a peering connection request on one side and an explicit accepter resource on the other
Cross-account peering requires the accepter account ID and, if applicable, a resource share or role trust for the acceptance step
rules.rationale: VPC peering is conceptually identical. AWS's separate request/accept step is the only structural difference from GCP's single bi-directional resource, and it is fully mechanical.

required_changes: GCP routes are VPC-level resources with no subnet association; AWS route tables are explicitly associated with one or more subnets, so each target subnet needs that association added
Each GCP route becomes a route entry inside the appropriate AWS route table rather than a standalone resource
rules.rationale: Route semantics are equivalent; the association model differs (VPC-level vs subnet-level route tables) but the translation is mechanical.

required_changes: Cloud Functions runtimes map to Lambda runtimes, but exact version availability differs and must be verified per language

Cloud Run's request-concurrency model (multiple concurrent requests per instance) has no Lambda equivalent: Lambda is one invocation per execution environment, which changes cost and cold-start characteristics for high-concurrency functions
GCP Secret Manager references in environment variables must be re-mapped to AWS Secrets Manager or Parameter Store references
manual_actions: Review and test function code for GCP client library calls (Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Firestore SDKs) that need replacement with AWS SDK equivalents
Validate event source mappings: GCS->S3 event notifications, Pub/Sub->SNS/SQS/EventBridge triggers, Cloud Endpoints->API Gateway
rules.rationale: FaaS-to-FaaS is conceptually clean but implementation details diverge significantly: event sources, concurrency model, and SDK surface all need per-function verification rather than a single mechanical rule.

required_changes: GKE-managed add-ons (kube-dns, GKE networking, Workload Identity) become explicit EKS add-ons (CoreDNS, VPC CNI, IAM Roles for Service Accounts) that must be installed and configured separately
GKE node pools map to EKS managed node groups with equivalent EC2 instance types
manual_actions: Migrate Kubernetes manifests: check for GKE-specific annotations, storage classes (GCP Persistent Disk CSI), and the GKE Ingress controller, all of which need EKS/AWS equivalents
Re-establish CI/CD pipeline to target the EKS cluster
rules.rationale: Both are managed Kubernetes. The control plane is equivalent; differences are in node management, networking add-ons, and storage integration, all of which are well-documented migration steps rather than open unknowns.

required_changes: Cloud Run service specifications map to an ECS task definition plus a service definition
Cloud Run's internal URLs or Service Directory-based discovery become ECS service discovery via AWS Cloud Map
manual_actions: Review container images for GCP SDK dependencies
Reconfigure load balancer and service-mesh integrations for the ALB + ECS service model
rules.rationale: Cloud Run to ECS/Fargate is a good fit for stateless HTTP workloads. Cloud Run's scale-to-zero, per-request billing has no ECS equivalent — Fargate's minimum-task-count model is a cost/behavior tradeoff rather than a translation gap.

required_changes: GCP Persistent Disk types map to EBS volume types: pd-balanced->gp3, pd-ssd->io2, pd-standard->st1
GCP resource policies (snapshot schedules) must be recreated as AWS Data Lifecycle Manager policies
rules.rationale: Block storage semantics are nearly identical. Volume type mapping is a lookup table. Data migration for attached volumes follows the owning compute instance's migration path.

required_changes: Filestore tiers (BASIC/HIGH_SCALE) map to EFS performance modes (general purpose/max I/O), but the capacity-provisioning models differ: Filestore is fixed-capacity, EFS is elastic
Filestore's NFS export options become EFS access points, which additionally support POSIX user/group enforcement not present in Filestore
manual_actions: Plan data migration: no native cross-cloud file system sync; use AWS DataSync or rsync over a temporary network path
rules.rationale: Both are managed NFS but feature sets diverge: Filestore is fixed-capacity and provisioned, EFS is elastic and serverless — moving from fixed to elastic capacity is a simplification, but the migration still requires an explicit data-transfer step.

required_changes: Firestore's document/collection model does not map 1:1 to DynamoDB's partition-key + sort-key model — the data model needs redesigning around DynamoDB access patterns, not a field-by-field translation
Firestore real-time listeners or Bigtable Change Streams become DynamoDB Streams
manual_actions: Redesign data model: Firestore document hierarchies or Bigtable row-key designs require rethinking as DynamoDB partition/sort-key patterns
Rewrite application data access layer: Firestore/Bigtable client library calls must be replaced with the DynamoDB SDK
rules.rationale: NoSQL-to-NoSQL is the highest-risk database migration: data models are fundamentally incompatible. Data model redesign and application-layer rewrites make this the platform's most manual database migration path, hence MANUAL.

required_changes: Memorystore for Redis maps directly to ElastiCache for Redis
Memorystore for Memcached maps to ElastiCache for Memcached
rules.rationale: Managed Redis/Memcached is semantically identical across clouds. Configuration parameters differ but every one has a direct ElastiCache equivalent.

required_changes: GCP IAM permissions must be mapped to AWS IAM policy actions (different namespace and granularity)
GCP IAM conditions (resource-attribute based) differ fundamentally from AWS IAM policy conditions (context-key based)
manual_actions: Security review of every translated policy: GCP and AWS permission models are not isomorphic
rules.rationale: IAM policies are the most security-sensitive translation in the platform. Automated mapping risks silent privilege escalation in either direction. MANUAL by design, matching the iam.role rule's stance.

required_changes: Secret values must be migrated securely (never through Terraform state or plan output)
GCP Secret Manager rotation functions (Cloud Functions-triggered) must be reconfigured as AWS Secrets Manager rotation Lambdas
manual_actions: Securely transfer secret values outside of Terraform using a migration script with appropriate access controls
rules.rationale: Secrets management semantics are very similar across clouds. The infrastructure (secret resource + IAM access policy) is mechanical; only the secret material itself requires an out-of-band, access-controlled transfer.

required_changes: Google-managed certificates become ACM certificates with DNS or email validation
DNS validation records must be recreated in Route 53
rules.rationale: Certificate management is functionally equivalent. ACM handles auto-renewal the same way Google-managed certificates do, so this is a like-for-like swap once DNS validation is re-pointed.

required_changes: URL map path rules become CloudFront cache behaviors
Cloud Run/Cloud Functions edge logic requires replacement with Lambda@Edge or CloudFront Functions
manual_actions: Review and test cache invalidation workflows
Validate custom error pages and redirect rules
rules.rationale: CDN functionality is equivalent but configuration models differ significantly. Simple static-asset distributions translate cleanly; edge-compute logic needs a genuine rewrite, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: NS records at the domain registrar must be updated to point to the new Route 53 name servers
GCP health-check-based routing policies become Route 53 health checks with routing policies (weighted/latency/failover)
manual_actions: Coordinate DNS cutover to minimize resolution disruption
rules.rationale: DNS zone management is semantically identical. The zone transfer is mechanical; the cutover is operational, hence the manual coordination step rather than an automation gap.

required_changes: Standard A/AAAA/CNAME records translate directly
GCP's geo/weighted routing policy records require Route 53 weighted or latency-based routing policies to reproduce the same behavior
rules.rationale: Standard DNS records are protocol-level identical. Advanced routing features (weighted, geolocation) have direct Route 53 equivalents, so no manual step is required.

required_changes: Pub/Sub subscriptions become SNS subscriptions
Pub/Sub subscription filters map to SNS message filtering policies
rules.rationale: Pub/sub messaging semantics are near-identical. Pub/Sub topics map 1:1 to SNS topics; subscription translation is rule-driven.

required_changes: Pub/Sub pull subscriptions map to SQS standard queues
Cloud Tasks with ordering, or Pub/Sub ordering keys, require SQS FIFO queues to preserve the same ordering guarantee
manual_actions: Evaluate whether SQS standard or FIFO better fits each queue's ordering and throughput requirements
rules.rationale: Queue semantics partially overlap: simple work queues translate well, but ordering guarantees and exactly-once delivery semantics don't line up 1:1 between Cloud Tasks/Pub-Sub and SQS, hence PARTIAL.

required_changes: GCP monitoring metric types map to CloudWatch metric namespaces
Notification channels (Pub/Sub, email, PagerDuty) become CloudWatch alarm actions (SNS, email, PagerDuty integration via SNS)
manual_actions: Validate alert thresholds against CloudWatch metric semantics, which may report differently than GCP Monitoring (e.g. different aggregation windows)
rules.rationale: Alerting concepts are equivalent but metric namespaces and evaluation semantics differ. Threshold validation is required per-metric rather than being assumable from the source configuration.

required_changes: Cloud Logging buckets become CloudWatch Log Groups
Log retention policies must be reconfigured to CloudWatch's retention-period model
rules.rationale: Centralized logging is functionally equivalent. CloudWatch auto-ingests from EC2/ECS/EKS the same way Cloud Logging auto-ingests from GCE/GKE, so this is a like-for-like swap.
